column16: A transcript of an oral history interview conducted by Dolores Smith with Dorothy Lannom on March 5, 1992, for the Friends of Stewart Library Oral History Project. The interview covers Dorothy Lannom's experiences in Grinnell, Iowa, during the Great Depression and World War II, focusing on her personal background, career as a home economics teacher, family life, local businesses such as glove and shoe factories, community organizations, social customs, and the impact of historical events on daily life in Grinnell during the 1930s and 1940s.

column6: Developmental Dilemmas: The Role of Power and Agency
column13: William D. Ferguson | Grinnell College | Cambridge University Press | UNU-WIDER | Kunal Sen | University of Manchester
column14: Development economics | Corruption | Power resources | Leadership | Institutional economics | Collective behavior | Political corruption | development economics | collective-action problems | power | agency | institutions | institutional entrepreneurs | social identities | resource conflict | political settlements | corruption | leadership | brokerage | inclusive development
column16: A scholarly monograph analyzing why developmental policies often fail, focusing on the interplay of power, agency, and institutional evolution in development economics. The work introduces a systematic framework for understanding collective-action problems, the role of powerful agents, and the significance of both formal and informal institutions in shaping developmental trajectories. It synthesizes literature from collective action theory, institutionalism, social conflict, and political settlements, and emphasizes the importance of institutional entrepreneurs, leadership, and brokerage in addressing developmental dilemmas such as social cleavage, resource conflict, and systemic corruption. Intended for advanced students, researchers, and policy analysts, the book combines theoretical exposition with case studies and game-theoretic models.

column6: Letter from mother to Staff Sgt James J. Ley, returned unclaimed
column13: Staff Sgt James J. Ley | 477th Bombardment Squad | Barksdale Field | Lakota, Iowa | San Francisco, California | Shreveport, Louisiana | David
column14: World War, 1939-1945 | Air mail service | Letter writing | Letter mail handling | World War II | air mail | returned mail | family letter | home front | Barksdale Field | 477th Bombardment Squad | Staff Sgt James J. Ley
column16: Handwritten letter from a mother to Staff Sgt James J. Ley, addressed to the 477th Bombardment Squad at Barksdale Field, Louisiana. The envelope bears multiple postal stamps and markings indicating it was sent via air mail from Lakota, Iowa on June 21, 1943, and was returned unclaimed after several forwarding attempts. The letter discusses family news, household activities, and well wishes, and is signed 'Mom.' The correspondence reflects home front life during World War II.

column6: Spaulding Model H Touring Car Advertisement, January 1, 1914
column13: Spaulding Mfg. Co. | Grinnell, Iowa | Gray and Davis
column14: Automobiles | Advertising | Touring cars (Racing automobiles) | Suspension | Sleeping cars (Railroads) | Gray family | Spaulding | Model H | touring car | sleeping car | automobile | advertisement | 1914 | Grinnell Iowa | Gray and Davis | motor suspension
column16: Advertisement and technical description for the Spaulding Model H touring car, highlighting radical body refinements and mechanical updates for 1914. The document describes the transformation of the touring car into a sleeping car via a hinged back seat, the inclusion of an electric reading lamp, three-point motor suspension, and streamlined body design. The car features a four-cylinder engine, left drive, center control, and Gray and Davis starter. Manufactured by Spaulding Mfg. Co., Grinnell, Iowa. The image depicts the Spaulding Model H, a five-passenger vehicle priced at $1,875.
